subject: Physics
q: Choose the wrong statement for zero error and zero correction.
question: Choose the wrong statement for zero error and zero correction.
options: If the zero of the vernier scale does not coincide with the zero of the main scale then the instrument is said to be having a zero error.
Zero correction has a magnitude equal to zero error but sign is opposite to that of the zero error.
Zero error is positive when the zero of vernier scale lies to the left of the zero of the main scale.
solution:
Zero error occurs in a measuring instrument when the instrument reads a non-zero value when the actual measured quantity is zero. For a Vernier Caliper, this happens when the jaws are closed but the zero mark of the Vernier scale does not align perfectly with the zero mark of the Main scale.

If the zeros do not coincide when the jaws are closed, the instrument has an inherent offset. This offset is defined as the Zero Error.

Thus, statement (a) is correct.

Zero correction is the value added to the observed reading to get the true reading.

Mathematically True Reading $=$ Observed Reading - Zero Error.

Therefore Zero Correction $=-($ Zero Error $)$.

This means the magnitude is the same, but the sign is reversed.

Thus, statement (b) is correct.

There are two types of zero error based on the relative position of the scales:

Positive Zero Error: This occurs when the zero of the Vernier scale lies to the right of the zero of the Main scale. The instrument reads a value larger than zero initially.

Negative Zero Error: This occurs when the zero of the Vernier scale lies to the left of the zero of the Main scale. The instrument reads a value below zero.

As per statement (c) zero error is positive when the Vernier zero is to the left. According to the standard convention, if the Vernier zero is to the left, the error is negative. Thus, statement (c) is incorrect.

Therefore, the wrong statement is (C).

Hence, the correct option is (C).

subject: Physics
q:
There are two Vernier callipers both of which have 1 cm divided into 10 equal divisions on the main scale. The Vernier scale of one of the callipers ($C_1$) has 10 equal divisions that correspond to 9 main scale divisions.
The Vernier scale of the other calliper ($C_2$) has 10 equal divisions that correspond to 11 main scale divisions.
The readings of the two callipers are shown in the figure. The measured values (in cm) by callipers $C_1$ and $C_2$, respectively, are
question:
There are two Vernier callipers both of which have 1 cm divided into 10 equal divisions on the main scale. The Vernier scale of one of the callipers ($C_1$) has 10 equal divisions that correspond to 9 main scale divisions.
The Vernier scale of the other calliper ($C_2$) has 10 equal divisions that correspond to 11 main scale divisions.
The readings of the two callipers are shown in the figure. The measured values (in cm) by callipers $C_1$ and $C_2$, respectively, are
solution:
For both callipers, 1 cm is divided into 10 divisions.

$$1\ \mathrm{MSD}=\frac{1\ \mathrm{cm}}{10}=0.1\ \mathrm{cm}$$

For Calliper $C_1$

Least Count is $\mathrm{LC}_1$

$$10\ \mathrm{VSD}=9\ \mathrm{MSD}$$

$$1\ \mathrm{VSD}=0.9\ \mathrm{MSD}$$

$$LC_1 = 1\ \mathrm{MSD} - 1\ \mathrm{VSD} = 1\ \mathrm{MSD} - 0.9\ \mathrm{MSD} = 0.1\ \mathrm{MSD}$$

$$LC_1 = 0.1 \times 0.1\ \mathrm{cm} = 0.01\ \mathrm{cm}$$

For $C_1$, the Vernier zero is after the $2.8\ \mathrm{cm}$ mark.

So, the main scale reading is $\mathrm{MSR} = 2.8\ \mathrm{cm}$

The 7th Vernier division coincides with a main scale mark.

So, the Vernier scale reading is $\mathrm{VSR} = (7 \times LC_1) = 7 \times 0.01\ \mathrm{cm} = 0.07\ \mathrm{cm}$

Thus, the reading of Vernier Calliper $C_1$ is $C_1 = \mathrm{MSR} + \mathrm{VSR} = 2.8\ \mathrm{cm} + 0.07\ \mathrm{cm} = 2.87\ \mathrm{cm}$

For Calliper $C_2$

Least Count is $LC_2$

$$10\ \mathrm{VSD} = 11\ \mathrm{MSD}$$

$$1\ \mathrm{VSD} = 1.1\ \mathrm{MSD}$$

$$LC_2 = 1\ \mathrm{MSD} - 1\ \mathrm{VSD} = 1\ \mathrm{MSD} - 1.1\ \mathrm{MSD} = -0.1\ \mathrm{MSD}$$

$$LC_2 = -0.1 \times 0.1\ \mathrm{cm} = -0.01\ \mathrm{cm}$$

For $C_2$, the Vernier zero is after the $2.8\ \mathrm{cm}$ mark.

So, the main scale reading is $\mathrm{MSR} = 2.8\ \mathrm{cm}$

The 7th Vernier division coincides with a main scale mark.

So, the Vernier scale reading is $\mathrm{VSR} = (7 \times LC_2) = 7 \times (-0.01)\mathrm{cm} = -0.07\ \mathrm{cm}$

Thus, the reading of Vernier Calliper $C_2$ is $C_2 = \mathrm{MSR} + \mathrm{VSR} = 2.8\ \mathrm{cm} - 0.07\ \mathrm{cm} = 2.83\ \mathrm{cm}$

Therefore, Calliper $C_1$ reading is $2.87\ \mathrm{cm}$ and Calliper $C_2$ reading is $2.83\ \mathrm{cm}$

Hence, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q:
In ordinary Vernier callipers, 10th division of the Vernier scale coincides with 9th division of the main scale. In a specially designed Vernier callipers the Vernier scale is so constructed that 10th division on it coincides with 11th division on the main scale. Each division on the main scale equals to 1 mm. The callipers have a zero error as shown in the figure-I. When the Vernier Calliper is used to measure a length, the concerned portion of its scale is shown in figure-II.

(A) Zero error in the callipers has magnitude 0.7 mm .

(B) The length being measured is $\mathbf{1 . 1 8 ~ c m}$.

(C) The length being measured is $\mathbf{1 . 2 2 ~ c m}$.

(D) Zero error in the callipers has magnitude 0.3 mm .
question:
In ordinary Vernier callipers, 10th division of the Vernier scale coincides with 9th division of the main scale. In a specially designed Vernier callipers the Vernier scale is so constructed that 10th division on it coincides with 11th division on the main scale. Each division on the main scale equals to 1 mm. The callipers have a zero error as shown in the figure-I. When the Vernier Calliper is used to measure a length, the concerned portion of its scale is shown in figure-II.

(A) Zero error in the callipers has magnitude 0.7 mm .

(B) The length being measured is $\mathbf{1 . 1 8 ~ c m}$.

(C) The length being measured is $\mathbf{1 . 2 2 ~ c m}$.

(D) Zero error in the callipers has magnitude 0.3 mm .
solution:
Main scale division (MSD) is given as $1 ext{ MSD} = 1 ext{ mm} = 0.1 ext{ cm}$.

It is given that 10 divisions on the Vernier scale coincide with 11 divisions on the main scale.

$$10 { VSD} = 11{ MSD}$$

$$1 { VSD} = 1.1 { MSD} = 1.1 { mm}$$

The magnitude of the least count is $LC = |1 { MSD} - 1{ VSD}| = |1{ mm} - 1.1{ mm}| = 0.1{ mm} = 0.01{ cm}$

In Figure-I, the jaws are closed and the zero of the Vernier scale is to the left of the main scale zero. This shows a negative zero error.

The 7th division of the Vernier scale coincides with a main scale mark.

For a scale where the zero is to the left, zero error $= -(n \times LC)$, where $n$ is the coinciding division.

$$\text{Zero Error} = -(7 \times 0.1\text{ mm}) = -0.7\text{ mm}$$

So, the magnitude of the zero error is $0.7{ mm}$. Hence, option (A) is correct.

Figure-II shows the measurement of an object. The zero of the Vernier scale is past the $1.1{ cm}$ $(11{ mm})$ mark.

$$\text{MSR} = 11\text{ mm}$$

The $5^{\text{th}}$ division of the Vernier scale coincides with a mark on the main scale.

So,

$$\text{Observed Reading} = \text{MSR} + (\text{VC} \times \text{LC})$$

$$\text{Observed Reading} = 11\text{ mm} + (5 \times 0.1\text{ mm}) = 11.5\text{ mm} = 1.15\text{ cm}$$

True Length $=$ Observed Reading - Zero Error

True Length $=1.15 \mathrm{~cm}-(-0.07 \mathrm{~cm})=1.22 \mathrm{~cm}$.

So the length being measured is 1.22 cm . Hence, option (C) is correct.

Therefore, the correct options are (A) and (C).

subject: Physics
q: The smallest division on the main scale of a vernier callipers is 1 mm, and 10 vernier divisions coincide with 9 main scale divisions. While measuring the diameter of a sphere, the zero mark of the vernier scale lies between 2.0 and 2.1 cm and the fifth division of the vernier scale coincides with a mark on main scale. Then diameter of the sphere is
question: The smallest division on the main scale of a vernier callipers is 1 mm, and 10 vernier divisions coincide with 9 main scale divisions. While measuring the diameter of a sphere, the zero mark of the vernier scale lies between 2.0 and 2.1 cm and the fifth division of the vernier scale coincides with a mark on main scale. Then diameter of the sphere is
solution:
The smallest division on the main scale is 1mm = 0.1 cm = main scale division (MSD)

10 vernier divisions coincide with 9 main scale divisions.

$$10\ \text{VSD} = 9\ \text{MSD} \rightarrow 1\ \text{VSD} = \frac{9}{10}\ \text{MSD} = 0.9\ \text{mm} = 0.09\ \text{cm}$$

The least count is the difference between one main scale division and one vernier scale division:

$$\text{LC} = 1\ \text{MSD} - 1\ \text{VSD}$$

$$\text{LC} = 0.1\ \text{cm} - 0.09\ \text{cm} = 0.01\ \text{cm}$$

The zero mark of the vernier scale lies between 2.0 cm and 2.1 cm. This means that the object has a length of at least 2.0 cm but less than 2.1 cm.

So, the main scale reading is $\text{MSR} = 2.0\ \text{cm}$

The fifth division of the vernier scale coincides precisely with a mark on the main scale.

$$\text{VC} = 5$$

So, total measured length is:

$$\text{Total Reading} = \text{MSR} + (\text{VC} \times \text{LC})$$

$$\text{Total Reading} = 2.0\ \text{cm} + (5 \times 0.01\ \text{cm})$$

$$\text{Total Reading} = 2.0\ \text{cm} + 0.05\ \text{cm} = 2.05\ \text{cm}$$

Therefore, the correct option is (A) 2.05 cm.

subject: Physics
q: The vernier constant of a vernier callipers is 0.1 mm and it has zero error of $-0.05$ cm. While measuring the diameter of a sphere, the main scale reading is $1.7$ cm and coinciding vernier division is $5$. The correct diameter will be $n \times 10^{-2}$ cm. Find $n$.
question: The vernier constant of a vernier callipers is 0.1 mm and it has zero error of $-0.05$ cm. While measuring the diameter of a sphere, the main scale reading is $1.7$ cm and coinciding vernier division is $5$. The correct diameter will be $n \times 10^{-2}$ cm. Find $n$.
solution:
Vernier constant (VC) is also known as the least count (LC).

$$VC = 0.1\ \mathrm{mm} = 0.110\ \mathrm{cm} = 0.01\ \mathrm{cm}$$

The zero error is negative zero error.

$$ZE = -0.05\ \mathrm{cm}$$

Main scale reading is given as $\mathrm{MSR} = 1.7\ \mathrm{cm}$

Vernier Coincidence $= 5$

The observed reading $= \mathrm{MSR} + (\text{Vernier Coincidence} \times VC)$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{Observed Reading} = 1.7\ \mathrm{cm} + (5 \times 0.01\ \mathrm{cm})$$

$$\text{Observed Reading} = 1.7\ \mathrm{cm} + 0.05\ \mathrm{cm} = 1.75\ \mathrm{cm}$$

The true value is always found by subtracting the zero error value (with its algebraic sign) from the observed reading:

$$\text{True Diameter} = \text{Observed Reading} - \text{Zero Error}$$

$$\text{True Diameter} = 1.75\ \mathrm{cm} - (-0.05\ \mathrm{cm})$$

$$\text{True Diameter} = 1.75\ \mathrm{cm} + 0.05\ \mathrm{cm} = 1.80\ \mathrm{cm}$$

$$1.80 = n \times 10^{-2}$$

$$1.80 = \frac{n}{100}$$

$$n = 1.80 \times 100 = 180$$

Therefore, the correct option is (A)

subject: Physics
q: The diagram shows part of the vernier scale of a pair of vernier callipers. Which reading is correct?
question: The diagram shows part of the vernier scale of a pair of vernier callipers. Which reading is correct?
solution:
The large marked steps are 3 cm and 4 cm.

There are 10 equal divisions between 3 cm and 4 cm, meaning each Main Scale Division represents:

$$1\ \text{MSD} = \frac{4\ \text{cm} - 3\ \text{cm}}{10} = \frac{1\ \text{cm}}{10} = 0.1\ \text{cm} = 1\ \text{mm}$$

The Vernier scale contains 10 equal divisions marked from 0 to 10.

10 Vernier divisions line up with 9 divisions on the main scale (0.9 cm).

$$10\ \text{VSD} = 9\ \text{MSD} \rightarrow 1\ \text{VSD} = 0.9\ \text{MSD} = 0.09\ \text{cm}$$

So, the Least Count is

$$\text{LC} = 1\ \text{MSD} - 1\ \text{VSD} = 0.1\ \text{cm} - 0.09\ \text{cm} = 0.01\ \text{cm}$$

The Vernier zero lies before the 3 cm mark, between the 2nd and 3rd minor divisions before 3 cm.

This means it has note crossed 3.0 cm but it has reached $2\ \text{cm} + (10 - 3) \times 0.1\ \text{cm} = 2\ \text{cm} + 0.7\ \text{cm} = 2.7\ \text{cm}$.

So, the main scale reading is $\text{MSR} = 2.7\ \text{cm}$

The 4th line of the Vernier scale aligns perfectly with a graduation line on the main scale.

$$\text{VC} = 4$$

$$\text{Total Reading} = \text{MSR} + (\text{VC} \times \text{LC})$$

$$\text{Total Reading} = 2.7\ \text{cm} + (4 \times 0.01\ \text{cm})$$

$$\text{Total Reading} = 2.7\ \text{cm} + 0.04\ \text{cm} = 2.74\ \text{cm}$$

Therefore, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: What is the reading of vernier scale shown is figure?
question: What is the reading of vernier scale shown is figure?
solution:
The main scale is marked as a mm Scale. The numbered markings are 50 and 60. The subdivisions between 50 and 60, there are 10 equal divisions. Therefore, each main scale division (MSD) represents:

$$1\ \mathrm{MSD} = 1\ \mathrm{mm}$$

The total number of divisions on the Vernier scale from 0 to the right edge is 10 equal divisions

10 Vernier scale divisions (VSD) usually coincide with 9 MSD.

$$\mathrm{LC} = 1\ \mathrm{MSD} - 1\ \mathrm{VSD}$$

$$10\ \mathrm{VSD} = 9\ \mathrm{MSD} \rightarrow 1\ \mathrm{VSD} = 0.9\ \mathrm{MSD} = 0.9\ \mathrm{mm}$$

$$\mathrm{LC} = 1\ \mathrm{mm} - 0.9\ \mathrm{mm} = 0.1\ \mathrm{mm}$$

The zero line of the Vernier scale has completely passed the 50 mm mark on main scale and it is between the 53 mm and 54 mm marks on the main scale.

Therefore, the main scale reading before the vernier zero is:

$$\mathrm{MSR} = 53\ \mathrm{mm}$$

The $2^{\mathrm{nd}}$ division on Vernier scale coincides with main scale division, so coinciding division $n = 2$.

$$\text{Total Reading} = \mathrm{MSR} + (\text{Coinciding Vernier Division} \times \text{Least Count})$$

So, Total Reading = $53\ \mathrm{mm} + (2 \times 0.1\ \mathrm{mm})$

$$\text{Total Reading} = 53\ \mathrm{mm} + 0.2\ \mathrm{mm} = 53.2\ \mathrm{mm}$$

Therefore, the correct option is (B)

subject: Physics
q: In a vernier callipers, ten divisions of the vernier scale is equal to the length of nine divisions on the main scale. If the smallest division on the main scale is half millimeter, then the vernier constant is:
question: In a vernier callipers, ten divisions of the vernier scale is equal to the length of nine divisions on the main scale. If the smallest division on the main scale is half millimeter, then the vernier constant is:
solution:
In vernier callipers, the vernier constant is the least count.

We use:

$$\text{Vernier constant} = 1 \text{ MSD} - 1 \text{ VSD}$$

Here, $10$ vernier scale divisions are equal to $9$ main scale divisions.

So,

$$1 \text{ VSD} = \frac{9}{10} \text{ MSD}$$

Given,

$$1 \text{ MSD} = 0.5 \text{ mm}$$

Then,

$$1 \text{ VSD} = \frac{9}{10} \times 0.5 = 0.45 \text{ mm}$$

Now,

$$\text{Vernier constant} = 1 \text{ MSD} - 1 \text{ VSD}$$

$$= 0.5 - 0.45 = 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

So the correct answer is:

$$\boxed{0.05 \text{ mm}}$$

Option C

subject: Physics
q: A vernier callipers has 20 divisions on the vernier scale, which coincide with 19 divisions on the main scale. The least count of the instrument is 0.1 mm. The main scale divisions are of
question: A vernier callipers has 20 divisions on the vernier scale, which coincide with 19 divisions on the main scale. The least count of the instrument is 0.1 mm. The main scale divisions are of
solution:
For a vernier calipers,

$$\text{Least Count} = 1\text{ MSD} - 1\text{ VSD}$$

Given that $20$ vernier scale divisions coincide with $19$ main scale divisions.

So,

$$20\text{ VSD} = 19\text{ MSD}$$

Hence,

$$1\text{ VSD} = \frac{19}{20}\text{ MSD}$$

Now least count is

$$\text{LC} = 1\text{ MSD} - 1\text{ VSD}$$

$$= 1\text{ MSD} - \frac{19}{20}\text{ MSD}$$

$$= \frac{1}{20}\text{ MSD}$$

Given,

$$\text{LC} = 0.1\text{ mm}$$

So,

$$\frac{1}{20}\text{ MSD} = 0.1\text{ mm}$$

$$1\text{ MSD} = 20 \times 0.1 = 2\text{ mm}$$

So the main scale division is

$$\boxed{2\text{ mm}}$$

Option C.

subject: Physics
q: A vernier callipers having 1 main scale division = 0.1 cm is designed to have a least count of 0.02 cm. If n be the number of divisions on vernier scale and m be the length of vernier scale, then
question: A vernier callipers having 1 main scale division = 0.1 cm is designed to have a least count of 0.02 cm. If n be the number of divisions on vernier scale and m be the length of vernier scale, then
solution:
Least count of a vernier callipers is given by

$$\text{Least count} = \text{1 main scale division} - \text{1 vernier scale division}$$

Here 1 main scale division (MSD) is $0.1$ cm and the least count we want is $0.02$ cm.

So if 1 vernier scale division is VSD, then

$$0.1 - \text{VSD} = 0.02$$

This gives

$$\text{VSD} = 0.08 \text{ cm}$$

Now there is a basic rule in vernier design: $n$ vernier divisions match with $(n-1)$ main scale divisions. So

$$n \times \text{VSD} = (n-1) \times \text{MSD}$$

Putting in the values,

$$n \times 0.08 = (n-1) \times 0.1$$

$$0.08n = 0.1n - 0.1$$

$$0.1 = 0.02n$$

$$n = 10$$

So there are 10 divisions on the vernier scale.

The length of the vernier scale is simply all 10 divisions taken together:

$$m = n \times \text{VSD} = 10 \times 0.08 = 0.8 \text{ cm}$$

So we get $n = 10$ and $m = 0.8$ cm, which matches Option C.

subject: Physics
q: The main scale of a vernier callipers reads in millimeter and its vernier is divided into $\mathbf{1 0}$ divisions which coincides with 9 divisions of the main scale. The reading for shown situation is found to be $\frac{12 x}{10} \mathrm{~mm}$. Find the value of $\mathbf{x}$.
question: The main scale of a vernier callipers reads in millimeter and its vernier is divided into $\mathbf{1 0}$ divisions which coincides with 9 divisions of the main scale. The reading for shown situation is found to be $\frac{12 x}{10} \mathrm{~mm}$. Find the value of $\mathbf{x}$.
solution:
A Vernier Calliper consists of two scales: a Main Scale (MS) and a Vernier Scale (VS).

It is given that the main scale reads in millimeters. Therefore, $1\ \mathrm{MSD} = 1\ \mathrm{mm}$.

It is given that 10 divisions of the vernier scale coincide with 9 divisions of the main scale.

$$10\ \mathrm{VSD} = 9\ \mathrm{MSD}$$

$$1\ \mathrm{VSD} = \frac{9}{10}\ \mathrm{MSD} = 0.9\ \mathrm{mm}$$

The Least Count is the smallest measurement that can be taken accurately with the instrument. It is the difference between one main scale division and one vernier scale division.

$$\mathrm{LC} = 1\ \mathrm{MSD} - 1\ \mathrm{VSD}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\mathrm{LC} = 1\ \mathrm{mm} - 0.9\ \mathrm{mm} = 0.1\ \mathrm{mm}$$

The fourth division of the Vernier scale aligns with a main scale mark. So the Vernier scale reading is,

$$\mathrm{VSR} = \mathrm{VC} \times \mathrm{LC} = 4 \times 0.1\ \mathrm{mm} = 0.4\ \mathrm{mm}$$

The zero of the Vernier scale is between 6th and 7th division on main scale. So, the main scale division for the measurement is 6.

Thus, the main scale reading is $n \times \mathrm{MSD} = 6 \times 1\ \mathrm{mm} = 6\ \mathrm{mm}$

So, the total observed reading is:

$$\text{Total observed reading} = \mathrm{MSR} + (\mathrm{VC} \times \mathrm{LC})$$

$$\text{Total observed reading} = 6\ \mathrm{mm} + (4 \times 0.1\ \mathrm{mm})$$

$$\text{Total observed reading} = 6\ \mathrm{mm} + 0.4\ \mathrm{mm} = 6.4\ \mathrm{mm}$$

The 0 mark of the Vernier scale is to the left of the main scale zero. So this is a negative zero error.

$$\mathrm{ZE} = -(10 - n) \times \mathrm{LC}$$

Where n is the division of Vernier scale which perfectly aligned with main scale division. $n = 2$

$$\mathrm{ZE} = -(10 - 2) \times 0.1\ \mathrm{mm} = -0.8\ \mathrm{mm}$$

The zero error should be subtracted from the observed reading to find the correct measurement,

The total measurement is observed reading $-$ zero error $= 6.4$ mm $-$ $(-0.8)$ mm $= 7.2$ mm

It is given that the reading is $\frac{12x}{10}$ mm.

$$7.2 = \frac{12x}{10}$$

$$\frac{72}{10} = \frac{12x}{10} \Rightarrow x = 6$$

Therefore, the value of $x$ is $6$.

subject: Physics
q: A vernier callipers has a least count of 0.1 mm. The main scale reading just to the left of the zero mark of the vernier scale is 10, and the zero division of the vernier scale exactly matches a division on the main scale. If one main scale division equals 1 mm, find the measured length in cm.
question: A vernier callipers has a least count of 0.1 mm. The main scale reading just to the left of the zero mark of the vernier scale is 10, and the zero division of the vernier scale exactly matches a division on the main scale. If one main scale division equals 1 mm, find the measured length in cm.
solution:
$$\text{Main Scale Reading} = 10 \times 1 \text{ mm} = 10 \text{ mm}$$

The formula to find the total measurement is:

$$\text{Total Reading} = \text{Main Scale Reading} + (\text{Vernier Scale Reading} \times \text{Least Count})$$

Now, put the values into the formula:

$$\text{Total Reading} = 10 \text{ mm} + (0 \times 0.1 \text{ mm})$$

$$\text{Total Reading} = 10 \text{ mm} + 0 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Total Reading} = 10 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Measured value} = 1 \text{ cm}$$

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment of simple pendulum, time period measured was 50 s for 25 oscillations when the length of the simple pendulum was taken as 100 cm . If the least count of stop watch is 0.1 s and that of meter scale is 0.01 cm , then the maximum possible percentage error in the calculated value of $\mathbf{g}$ is P. Then Find the value of 100P.
question: In an experiment of simple pendulum, time period measured was 50 s for 25 oscillations when the length of the simple pendulum was taken as 100 cm . If the least count of stop watch is 0.1 s and that of meter scale is 0.01 cm , then the maximum possible percentage error in the calculated value of $\mathbf{g}$ is P. Then Find the value of 100P.
solution:
The formula for the time period ($T$) of a simple pendulum of effective length $L$ executing small-angle simple harmonic motion is:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$$

$$T^2 = 4\pi^2 \frac{L}{g}$$

$$g = \frac{4\pi^2 L}{T^2}$$

The total time measured for $N$ oscillations is $t$. The time period for a single oscillation is:

$$T = \frac{t}{N}$$

Substitute this into expression for $g$:

$$g = 4\pi^2 \frac{L}{\left(\frac{t}{N}\right)^2} = 4\pi^2 N^2 \frac{L}{t^2}$$

Taking the natural logarithm ($\ln$) on both sides of the equation:

$$\ln(g) = \ln(4\pi^2 N^2) + \ln(L) - \ln(t^2)$$

$$\ln(g) = \ln(4\pi^2 N^2) + \ln(L) - 2\ln(t)$$

Differentiating both sides to find the small fractional changes (errors):

$$\frac{\Delta g}{g} = 0 + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{2\Delta t}{t}$$

The maximum possible absolute error,

$$\frac{\Delta g}{g} = \frac{\Delta L}{L} + \frac{2\Delta t}{t}$$

$$\left(\frac{\Delta g}{g} \times 100\right) = \left(\frac{\Delta L}{L} \times 100\right) + 2\left(\frac{\Delta t}{t} \times 100\right)$$

The length is given as $L = 100\ \text{cm}$

Absolute error in length $(\Delta L)$ corresponds to the least count of the scale, which is $0.01\ \text{cm}$.

Total measured time $t = 50\ \text{s}$

Absolute error in time $(\Delta t)$ corresponds to the least count of the stopwatch, which is $0.1\ \text{s}$.

Substituting the values,

$$\frac{\Delta g}{g} \times 100 = \left(\frac{0.01}{100} \times 100\right) + 2\left(\frac{0.1}{50} \times 100\right)$$

$$\frac{\Delta g}{g} \times 100 = 0.01\% + 2(0.2\%)$$

$$\frac{\Delta g}{g} \times 100 = 0.01\% + 0.4\% = 0.41\%$$

Therefore, the maximum possible percentage error in the calculated value of $g$ is, P = $0.41\%$.

$$\therefore $$ 100P = 41.

subject: Physics
q: The density of a cube is calculate D by measuring its mass and the length of its sides. If the maximum errors in the measurement of mass and length are $3 \%$ and $2 \%$ respectively, then the maximum error in the value of density is $\_\_\_\_$ $\%$.
question: The density of a cube is calculate D by measuring its mass and the length of its sides. If the maximum errors in the measurement of mass and length are $3 \%$ and $2 \%$ respectively, then the maximum error in the value of density is $\_\_\_\_$ $\%$.
solution:
$$d = \frac{m}{V}$$

Since the volume of a cube is $l^3$, the equation for density becomes:

$$d = \frac{m}{l^3}$$

$$\frac{\Delta d}{d} \times 100 = \left( \frac{\Delta m}{m} \times 100 \right) + 3 \left( \frac{\Delta l}{l} \times 100 \right)$$

$$\frac{\Delta d}{d} \times 100 = 3\% + 3(2\%)$$

$$\frac{\Delta d}{d} \times 100 = 3\% + 6\%$$

$$\frac{\Delta d}{d} \times 100 = 9\%$$

The maximum error in the value of density is $9\%$.

subject: Physics
q: The radius of a sphere is measured to be $5.3 \pm 0.1 \mathrm{~cm}$. Calculate percentage error in volume. Round off to nearest integer.
question: The radius of a sphere is measured to be $5.3 \pm 0.1 \mathrm{~cm}$. Calculate percentage error in volume. Round off to nearest integer.
solution:
For a sphere, $$ V=\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 $$

When we find percentage error, we use the rule for powers:

$$ \frac{\Delta V}{V}=3\frac{\Delta r}{r} $$

Here,

$$ r=5.3\text{ cm}, \qquad \Delta r=0.1\text{ cm} $$

So,

$$ \frac{\Delta V}{V}=3\left(\frac{0.1}{5.3}\right) $$

$$ \frac{\Delta V}{V}=3(0.01887)\approx 0.0566 $$

Percentage error in volume:

$$ 0.0566\times 100 = 5.66\% $$

Rounded to the nearest integer, the percentage error is

$$ \boxed{6\%} $$
